coredata.dc:title: Effect of composite additives in fine water mist on suppressing thermal runaway in lithium batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Qinkun, Xu
Xin, Xia
Tingting, Tian
coredata.dc:description: To address the safety hazards of lithium battery fires, the limitations of low cooling efficiency of fine water mist fire-extinguishing technology, and the research gap regarding composite additives, this study conducted experiments on the suppression of lithium battery fires by fine water mist containing different additives based on a self-built experimental platform. The study used NCM ternary lithium batteries as the research object and triggered thermal runaway through external heating. The experimental results indicate that the higher the battery SOC (State of Charge), the earlier the thermal runaway is triggered, the higher the peak temperature, and the more intense the combustion phenomena. Under the action of fine water mist, the thermal runaway process of lithium batteries can be divided into four stages, but reignition phenomena still occurs. Each additive has an optimal mass fraction (0.15 % for FeCl2, 2.5 % for sodium lactate, and 0.3 % for both urea and Tween 20). Among them, FeCl2 and sodium lactate perform excellently in suppressing the temperature rise during thermal runaway, while urea and Tween 20 have more advantages in enhancing cooling performance. The composite additives demonstrate the best overall performance, especially the combinations of FeCl2 + Tween 20 and sodium lactate + Tween 20, which can reduce the maximum temperature to about 650℃ (an improvement of about 35 % in suppression effect compared with pure water mist) and effectively prevent reignition. This study provides theoretical support and technical references for the safety design, fire prevention, and emergency response of lithium batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Water mist
Additives
Thermal runaway

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The impact of SOC on thermal runaway in lithium batteries and the suppression effect of fine water mist on lithium battery fires
Study on the suppression effect of fine water mist containing a single additive on lithium battery fires
Study on the inhibitory effect of composite additives on lithium battery fire

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Thermal runaway process of lithium batteries at different SOC levels.
Fine water mist suppresses thermal runaway in lithium batteries.
Temperature change of lithium battery thermal runaway under FeCl2 and sodium lactate interaction.
Changes in lithium battery thermal runaway temperature under the influence of urea and Tween 20.
Effect of FeCl2 on sodium lactate.
Inhibitory Effects of Urea and Tween 20.
Temperature changes in lithium battery fires under fine water mist with composite additives.
Temperature changes during the intense thermal runaway stage under fine water mist with composite additives.
Peak temperature of lithium battery fires under different additive application strategies.

row.entry: Lithium Nickel Cobalt Manganese Oxide (NCM, LiNi₀.₅Co₀.₃Mn₀.₂O₂)
Continuous release of flue gas and combustion
Release large amounts of smoke and jet flames
Flammable and accompanied by large amounts of smoke

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Key Parameters in Thermal Runaway Process.

row.entry: Average heating rate during thermal runaway /°C/s

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Lithium battery reignition under different operating conditions.

head.ce:title: Effect of composite additives in fine water mist on suppressing thermal runaway in lithium batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment and Resources Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mian yang, Sichuan 621010, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environment and Resources Southwest University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environment and Resources Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mian yang, Sichuan, 621010, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To address the safety hazards of lithium battery fires, the limitations of low cooling efficiency of fine water mist fire-extinguishing technology, and the research gap regarding composite additives, this study conducted experiments on the suppression of lithium battery fires by fine water mist containing different additives based on a self-built experimental platform. The study used NCM ternary lithium batteries as the research object and triggered thermal runaway through external heating. The experimental results indicate that the higher the battery SOC (State of Charge), the earlier the thermal runaway is triggered, the higher the peak temperature, and the more intense the combustion phenomena. Under the action of fine water mist, the thermal runaway process of lithium batteries can be divided into four stages, but reignition phenomena still occurs. Each additive has an optimal mass fraction (0.15 % for FeCl2, 2.5 % for sodium lactate, and 0.3 % for both urea and Tween 20). Among them, FeCl2 and sodium lactate perform excellently in suppressing the temperature rise during thermal runaway, while urea and Tween 20 have more advantages in enhancing cooling performance. The composite additives demonstrate the best overall performance, especially the combinations of FeCl2 + Tween 20 and sodium lactate + Tween 20, which can reduce the maximum temperature to about 650℃ (an improvement of about 35 % in suppression effect compared with pure water mist) and effectively prevent reignition. This study provides theoretical support and technical references for the safety design, fire prevention, and emergency response of lithium batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the 1970s, lithium batteries have been widely used in applications such as electric vehicles and portable consumer electronics owing to their high energy density, long cycle life, and environmental friendliness [1]. However, with the expansion of their application scale, the safety concerns of lithium batteries have become increasingly prominent [2]. Fire is a complex phenomenon [3], and lithium battery fires are no exception. Under abusive conditions such as external heating, overcharging, mechanical deformation, or internal short-circuiting, lithium batteries undergo a series of exothermic reactions [4], ultimately leading to thermal runaway. This results in the release of a large amount of heat, emission of toxic gases, and phenomena such as flame ejection, explosion, and sustained combustion, which pose a serious threat to life, property, and the environment [5].

However, the unique structural characteristics and combustion behavior of lithium batteries make traditional fire extinguishants ineffective. Dry powder and carbon dioxide extinguishants cannot effectively penetrate the battery casing to cool the internal heat source. Traditional water spray is also ineffective due to low heat absorption efficiency and poor surface adhesion, making it difficult to achieve adequate cooling [6]. In contrast, fine water mist has attracted widespread attention as a highly promising suppression method, and many scholars have conducted research in this area. Zhao et al. [7]compared the suppression effects of dry powder and water on lithium battery fires and concluded that water mist has the highest cooling efficiency. Liu et al. [8] experimentally investigated the suppression effect of fine water mist on thermal runaway. The results showed that applying fine water mist before the critical temperature can effectively control thermal runaway, while thermal runaway cannot be suppressed once the critical temperature is exceeded. Liu [9] proposed and verified a novel fine water mist control strategy. The results indicated that fine water mist has excellent cooling performance and can control the risk of thermal runaway even under extreme conditions. He also found that the cooling process mainly relies on the evaporation of fine water mist and proposed a cooling coefficient to quantify the cooling effect. These studies have confirmed the effectiveness of fine water mist in suppressing lithium battery fires, but the cooling effect of fine water mist still needs to be further improved. Liu et al. [10] investigated the fire-extinguishing effects of fine water mist at different intensities and derived the relationship between the pressure of fine water mist and the extinguishing time. In addition to this, the application method of fine water mist also affects the cooling effect of lithium batteries. Said A O et al. [11] conducted experiments using fine water mist at rates of 1.0 g/s and 1.6 g/s. Under both fine water mist delivery rates, the flames associated with the combustion of battery ejecta were suppressed, reducing the combustion efficiency to below 50 %. Zhang [12] enhanced the suppression efficiency of lithium battery fires using an intermittent spray method that controls the flow rate, revealing that the cooling effect is better with more pulses and shorter durations in the intermittent spray pattern. It is indeed feasible to improve the cooling effect of fine water mist by applying it in different ways. However, most of these methods require the assistance of various external conditions and involve overly complicated operating procedures. In comparison, the method of adding additives to fine water mist is much simpler. Xu [13] evaluated the cooling performance of different additives in water mist for suppressing thermal runaway of lithium batteries. The experimental results showed that heptanol additives can significantly enhance the heat transfer efficiency of water mist, while FS-51 additives exhibit heat transfer inhibition characteristics. There is a significant correlation between the cooling performance of additives and their surface tension and foaming properties. Additives with lower surface tension and weaker foaming properties show better cooling effects. Yuan [14] investigated the micelle encapsulant F-500, discussing the fire-extinguishing effects and cooling capabilities of the F-500 solution and fine water mist. It was found that H₂ is the main gas released during thermal runaway. Zhu et al. [15]studied the impact of fine water mist containing surfactants on gas explosions in lithium battery fires. The results showed that adding no more than 5 % surfactant can effectively extinguish the fire and significantly suppress explosions. Zhou et al. [16]prepared low-conductivity fine water mist by adding nonionic surfactants and found that it cannot only effectively inhibit the propagation of thermal runaway among lithium batteries, but also reduce the damage of fine water mist to unaffected cells.

Existing research has confirmed the cooling potential of fine water mist for lithium battery fires, but there are still limitations. First, when used alone, fine water mist has the disadvantage of insufficient cooling capacity and cannot eliminate the residual heat inside the battery, leading to a higher likelihood of reignition. Second, current research on additives mostly focuses on single components (such as individual surfactants or individual salts) and does not explore the synergistic effects of composite additives. The dual advantages of "chemical inhibition of temperature rise" and "physical enhancement of cooling" have not been integrated. This study uses the NCM ternary lithium battery as the research object and employs controllable external heating to simulate real thermal abuse scenarios. First, the optimal mass fraction of the additives was determined. Then, a composite additive system combining "chemical and physical types" was constructed. Finally, by analyzing the temperature changes during thermal runaway and the occurrence of reignition, the advantages of composite additives were verified, breaking through the limitations of single additives that "either excel in temperature rise inhibition or in enhanced cooling". The view that "the effectiveness of composite additives depends on the match between 'mode of action and response rhythm'" was put forward. This theoretically fills the gap in the synergistic inhibition of lithium battery thermal runaway by composite additives, and the research results are expected to provide theoretical support for lithium battery safety design, fire prevention and control strategies, and actual emergency fire response.

The self-built experimental platform and associated equipment of this study are illustrated in Fig. 1, including a laboratory, a water mist system, a temperature acquisition device, a heating device, and a camera. The combustion chamber used in the experiment, with dimensions of 0.8 m× 0.8 m× 1.0 m, has one side made of transparent high-temperature-resistant insulating board, while the other sides are made of galvanized steel plates with a thickness of 0.5 cm. The water mist system consists of a tap water source and a general-purpose mobile fine water mist device, with the nozzle located 1.5 m above the battery. The data acquisition system is composed of a computer, a 7018-temperature acquisition module, and thermocouples. The temperature acquisition module has a precision of 0.1 s, and the thermocouples have a temperature measurement range of 0–1100℃ with an accuracy of ±0.1℃. A SONY high-definition camera is used for image recording.

To eliminate the influence of battery cycle counts on the experiment [17], three charge-discharge cycles were performed prior to the experiment. The battery was connected to the BMS (Battery Management System) and monitoring equipment to record parameters such including the initial voltage and capacity of the battery. The battery was subjected to charge and discharge cycles at a constant current and voltage to reach the predetermined initial state of charge. The parameters of the lithium batteries used in the experiment are shown in Table 1 below:

The fine water mist fire extinguishing device used in the experiment is the commonly used QWLB12/0.75-A type two-phase flow backpack water mist fire extinguishing device in firefighting. During the experiment, to prevent the residues of lithium batteries from falling into the heating device during heating and affecting the experimental results, a steel plate was heated under the lithium battery, and the heat was then transferred to the lithium battery. The experimental steps are as follows:
(1)
Check whether the experimental equipment (heating device, temperature sensors, data acquisition system) is working properly, and ensure that the battery fixing device is stable and usable to prevent the battery from moving or falling off during the experiment.
(2)
Place the battery and turn on the heating device to heat (heating power is 500 W, and the initial ambient temperature of the laboratory is 25 ± 3℃).
(3)
Stop heating when the battery swells, and smoke appears, and spray fine water mist until the open flame is extinguished.
(4)
Wait for the battery to cool down (the temperature acquisition time is 1000 s).
(5)
To ensure the reliability of the experimental data, each group of experiments is repeated three times.

The experimental design is shown in Table 2. The mass fractions of the additives in the experiment were designed with reference to previous scholars' research on these additives. However, due to the different experimental objects, a rough range was first determined, and then the range was gradually narrowed down using the binary method until the optimal mass fraction was found. In the composite additive experiments, the mass fraction of each additive was set to its optimal value. During the experiment, it was found that the SOC of the battery had a great influence on the combustion phenomena of lithium batteries. To eliminate the influence of SOC on subsequent experiments and make the experimental results more evident, the state of charge of the batteries in the subsequent additive experiments was set to 100 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The impact of SOC on thermal runaway in lithium batteries and the suppression effect of fine water mist on lithium battery fires
ce:section.ce:para: The combustion behavior of lithium batteries varies with the state of charge [18]. In general, three main types of combustion behaviors can be observed: the first is continuous smoke emission; the second is flame ejection accompanied by a large amount of smoke; and the third is deflagration, which releases a large amount of flame [19]. Fig. 2 illustrates the combustion process of lithium batteries at different states of charge (SOC). When the battery is at 0 % SOC, no combustion occurs. Only a small amount of smoke is generated on both sides of the battery, and the temperature variation throughout the process is minimal. When the SOC increases to 25 %, the thermal runaway moment is advanced from 712 s at 0 % SOC to 467 s. At this point, the battery not only releases smoke but also exhibits combustion phenomena. At 50 % SOC, the combustion phenomenon becomes more pronounced, with a significant amount of smoke and jet flames produced. Further increasing the SOC advances the thermal runaway trigger time and intensifies the combustion process, resulting in a large amount of smoke and deflagration. At 100 % SOC, deflagration occurs with a large amount of smoke, similar to the 75 % SOC condition, but with a higher peak temperature. At the same time, it was noticed that after the battery temperature exceeds 180℃, the thermal runaway of the battery will change violently. In the subsequent experiments, in order to avoid missing the timing of water mist application, while observing the experimental phenomena, attention was also paid to the change of battery temperature. When the battery temperature reached 180℃, the heating was stopped, and the water mist generating device was turned on. Table 3 summarizes the combustion phenomena of lithium batteries at different SOC.

These phenomena in the experiments are attributed to the fact that a higher SOC corresponds to greater lithium-ion chemical energy [20], providing more energy for exothermic reactions. The low thermal stability of the battery's cathode material leads to decomposition and oxygen release. Additionally, the strong reducing nature of the anode initiates exothermic reactions with the electrolyte at a lower temperature. These factors collectively accelerate exothermic reactions and overlap various exothermic side reactions (such as SEI film decomposition, cathode decomposition, and anode-electrolyte reactions). This ultimately leads to a sharp temperature rise and intense combustion or even explosion. It is evident that batteries with high SOC will exhibit more violent energy release under thermal runaway conditions, are more prone to thermal runaway, and will have significantly increased combustion intensity.

Fig. 3 shows the temperature change process of lithium battery thermal runaway with the application of fine water mist. It can be observed that under the action of fine water mist, the temperature shows a downward trend, and the peak temperature of battery thermal runaway is also lower compared to when fine water mist is not applied. Based on the temperature changes, this process can be roughly divided into four stages.
(1)
Energy Accumulation Stage: Triggering thermal runaway in lithium batteries requires reaching a specific temperature condition [21]. During this stage, the battery's temperature rise rate gradually increases, and heat continues to accumulate.
(2)
Thermal Runaway Suppression Stage: At this point, the internal temperature of the battery is sufficient to trigger thermal runaway. Intense reactions occur inside the battery, causing a sharp increase in temperature, accompanied by dense smoke and flame ejection. After applying fine water mist, it covers the surface of the battery, forming a liquid film that reduces the heat dissipation to the surrounding environment and lowers the combustion intensity. Since the cooling rate of the fine water mist exceeds the battery's temperature rise rate at this time, the battery temperature quickly drops after reaching its peak.
(3)
Reignition Stage: After the application of fine water mist is stopped, the battery temperature rebounds, reaching a maximum of 867℃. The main reason for the temperature rebound is the residual heat inside the battery and the uneven cooling effect caused by the temperature difference between the inside and outside of the battery [22]. During the application of fine water mist, the battery cools down quickly, but the inside, due to its poor thermal conductivity, fails to dissipate heat in time, resulting in a "cold outside, hot inside" state. Once the application of fine water mist is stopped, the residual heat inside is conducted to the surface, causing the temperature to rise again, which leads to reignition.
(4)
Natural Cooling Stage: As the fine water mist continues to evaporate and absorb heat, and with the effect of the surrounding cold air, the battery temperature gradually decreases until it stabilizes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the suppression effect of fine water mist containing a single additive on lithium battery fires
ce:section.ce:para: The occurrence of thermal runaway in lithium batteries mainly originates from the complex exothermic reactions inside them. When the heat released by the reactions exceeds the system's heat dissipation capacity, it leads to a continuous increase in battery temperature, accompanied by the ejection of a large amount of combustible materials [23]. This process is significantly different from conventional fires, making it difficult to measure the suppression effect of fine water mist on lithium battery thermal runaway with a unified standard. To address this, this study sets the highest temperature during thermal runaway and the duration of intense thermal runaway as key evaluation indicators to analyze the differences in suppression effects under different mass fractions. In addition, as mentioned earlier, after the fine water mist is stopped, the residual heat inside the battery continues to be released, causing the temperature rise rate to exceed the cooling rate again, leading to a temperature rebound, which means entering the reignition stage. This stage still belongs to the intense thermal runaway process. Based on this, this study defines the period from the first occurrence of thermal runaway to the temperature drop after reignition as the "intense thermal runaway stage."

Fig. 4 shows the changes in the highest temperature during thermal runaway and the duration of intense thermal runaway under the action of FeCl2 and sodium lactate when the mass fraction is changed. Regarding the highest temperature, it is about 915℃ at a mass fraction of 0.2 %, and it drops to the lowest when increased to 0.25 %, after which the temperature rises again. Regarding the duration of intense thermal runaway, there is a pattern of "first decreasing and then increasing." It is about 171 s at 0.1 %, and after increasing the mass fraction, the time becomes shorter, reaching the shortest at 0.15 %. However, the duration begins to gradually extend afterwards, increasing to 253 s at 0.30 %. At the same time, it can be found that during the thermal runaway process, the high-temperature duration of lithium batteries is significantly longer at mass fractions of 0.3 %, 0.25 %, and 0.1 %. The temperature drop rate is relatively faster at 0.15 %. As a chemically reactive additive, FeCl2 interrupts chain reactions by ionizing Fe²⁺ to combine with free radicals. However, when the mass fraction is too low, the released Fe²⁺ ions are insufficient to fully intervene in the reaction. On the other hand, when the mass fraction is too high, the excess Fe²⁺ may catalyze some side reactions, such as the partial decomposition of the electrolyte, which in turn leads to heat regeneration and weakens the cooling and suppression efficiency. In this study, at a mass fraction of 0.15 %, FeCl2 can effectively inhibit the generation of high temperatures and significantly compress the duration of the intense reaction phase. Considering these factors comprehensively, the optimal mass fraction of FeCl2 is 0.15 %.

For sodium lactate, as the mass fraction of the additive increases, the battery's highest temperature shows a "first stable then rising" trend. Initially, it stays between 830–850℃ with little change, but then rises sharply at 3.5 %. The duration of thermal runaway reaches its minimum of 203 s at 2.5 %, but then quickly rises again if the mass fraction is further decreased or increased. Sodium lactate, a sodium salt additive, can release Na⁺ ions that interact with free radicals generated during combustion, interrupting the reaction's transmission process [24]. This chemical mechanism helps reduce the energy release rate during thermal runaway and slows the temperature rise. The specific reaction pathways are as follows [25]:
Na
+
+
OH
−
→ NaOH
NaOH +
H
+
→
Na
+
+
H
2
O
NaOH +
OH
−
→ Na
O
−
+
H
2
O
Na
O
−
+
H
+
→
Na
+
+
OH
−

When the mass fraction of sodium lactate is too low, the ion concentration in the solution is insufficient to effectively exert its chemical inhibition, thus reducing the cooling and suppression effect. When the mass fraction is too high, the solution viscosity increases [26], degrading the fine water mist atomization performance. Moreover, sodium lactate at high concentrations may thermally decompose at high temperatures [27], producing flammable by-products that could intensify local oxidation and form new combustion sources, reducing overall fire - extinguishing efficiency. Although sodium lactate at 2.5 % mass fraction did not achieve the lowest temperature, it significantly shortened the intense phase duration and had better cooling effects. Therefore, the optimal mass fraction of sodium lactate is 2.5 %.

Fig. 5 shows the changes in the suppression effects of urea and Tween 20 when the mass fraction is altered. The suppression effect of urea is highly sensitive to changes in mass fraction, with distinct characteristics. Specifically, the highest temperature shows a significant downward trend. At 0.1 % mass fraction, the highest temperature is around 970℃, reaching the lowest at 0.3 %. After the mass fraction exceeds 0.3 %, the temperature rises sharply. The duration of intense thermal runaway is synchronized with the changes in the highest temperature. At 0.1 %, the duration is about 140 s, reaching the shortest at 0.3 %. When the mass fraction exceeds 0.3 %, the duration extends. Urea mainly inhibits lithium battery fires through two mechanisms. On the one hand, urea is prone to decomposition when heated in a high - temperature environment, and the decomposition process can absorb a large amount of heat [28]. On the other hand, the decomposition produces inert gases, which reduce the oxygen content in the environment. Both aspects accelerate flame extinction and lower the surface temperature of lithium batteries. However, when the mass fraction is too low, the total heat absorbed by the endothermic decomposition reaction is limited, and it cannot quickly reduce the temperature of the battery and high - temperature gases. At the same time, the amount of inert gas produced is too small to effectively extinguish the flame and reduce the oxygen content in the environment. When the mass fraction is too high, the viscosity of the urea solution increases, and high - viscosity liquids are more difficult to atomize into fine droplets. Overall, the optimal mass fraction of urea is 0.3 %.

Tween 20 mainly enhances the cooling effect of fine water mist by improving its wettability and coverage [29]. The highest temperature in the graph shows a clear downward trend in the 0.1 % - 0.3 % range, with little change between 0.1 % and 0.2 %, and reaching the lowest value at 0.3 %. In the 0.4 % - 0.5 % range, the temperature is basically stable with fluctuations less than 0.6 %. The duration of thermal runaway is about 180 s at 0.1 % and reaches the shortest at 0.3 %. There is a "critical percentage" for the surface tension reduction ability of Tween 20 [30]. Between 0.1 % and 0.3 %, as the mass fraction increases, molecules gradually adsorb at the water - air interface, the surface tension continues to decrease, the atomization effect of fine water mist is gradually optimized, and the cooling efficiency is enhanced. When the mass fraction exceeds 0.3 %, the surface tension is reduced to the minimum and no longer changes with the increase in mass fraction. The atomization effect is stable, and the temperature enters a plateau period without significant further decline. At a mass fraction of 0.3 %, Tween 20 has achieved optimal atomization and coverage. The cooling rate of fine water mist and its ability to isolate heat from the battery are maximized. This can effectively control the peak temperature and continuously remove residual heat to avoid heat accumulation during the reignition stage, thus keeping the duration of intense thermal runaway at the optimal level. Further increasing the mass fraction does not change the surface tension, and there is no additional improvement in atomization and cooling performance. Therefore, the duration remains stable. Overall, the optimal mass fraction of Tween 20 is 0.3 %.

Table 4 summarizes the key parameters of the lithium battery thermal runaway process under the action of fine water mist and various additives. Here, to quickly judge the overall targeted effects of each additive in the "temperature rise stage" and "cooling stage," six indicators are selected based on the evolution law of lithium battery thermal runaway to form the evaluation parameters. First, it is necessary to clarify the "direction of superiority and inferiority" of the indicators to ensure the consistency of the analysis logic. Indicators in the temperature rise stage (the smaller the value, the better the inhibition effect): the highest temperature during thermal runaway (℃), the average temperature rise rate during thermal runaway (℃/s), and the maximum temperature rise rate (℃/s), which directly reflect the ability of additives to block the triggering and spread of thermal runaway; indicators in the cooling stage (the larger the value, the better the promotion effect): the average cooling rate (℃/s) and the maximum cooling rate (℃/s). The cooling duration here (s) refers to the time it takes for the peak temperature to drop to a safe threshold and should be a negative indicator. Negative indicators need to be processed positively to ensure consistency with other positive indicators.

Due to the significant differences in the scales of the indicators, direct comparison would lead to "large numerical indicators overshadowing the differences of small numerical indicators" (for example, the fluctuation range of the highest temperature during thermal runaway can reach 100–200℃, while the fluctuation of the cooling rate is only 2–5℃/s). Therefore, the range normalization method is adopted to normalize the original data.

Fig. 6 shows the comparison of the suppression effects of FeCl2 and sodium lactate at their optimal mass fractions. The suppression effect of FeCl2 is characterized by "strong inhibition during the temperature rise stage," which is far more effective in inhibiting the temperature rise process than in promoting the cooling stage. The Fe²⁺ ions dissociated from FeCl2 have strong reducing properties [31], which can react with free radicals such as •OH and O• generated during thermal runaway. Additionally, Fe²⁺ can react with PF₅, a decomposition product of LiPF6 in the electrolyte, to inhibit the continuous exothermic decomposition of the electrolyte, thereby reducing the heat generation rate at the source. Sodium lactate, similar to FeCl2, exhibits the characteristic of "strong inhibition during the temperature rise stage and weak promotion during the cooling stage." The Na⁺ ions dissociated from sodium lactate have a free - radical - capturing role, and the hydroxyl group in the lactate can react with •OH to form the stable CH₃CH(O•) COO⁻, further enhancing the ability to interrupt chain reactions. Therefore, the action of FeCl2 and sodium lactate is mainly reflected in the inhibition of the temperature rise process.

Fig. 7 shows the suppression effect of urea and Tween 20. Under the action of urea, the average temperature rise rate is significantly low, but it has an excellent promotion effect on various indicators during the cooling process. Urea decomposes at high temperatures to generate inert gases, which can dilute the oxygen concentration while absorbing heat during decomposition to inhibit temperature rise. However, since the decomposition products have no coverage ability, they cannot block the continuous heat release from inside the battery, thus limiting the inhibition effect during the temperature rise stage. Tween 20 also shows a better promotion effect on the cooling process than on the temperature rise process. This is because Tween 20 can reduce the droplet size of fine water mist [32], thereby increasing the heat transfer efficiency on the battery surface. At the same time, the uniformity of droplet coverage is improved, effectively avoiding local heat residue and accelerating heat removal. Moreover, Tween 20 is biodegradable and is more suitable for scenarios with high environmental protection requirements. Therefore, urea and Tween 20 are more focused on promoting the cooling process.

In summary, it can be seen that there are obvious differences in the fields of effectiveness of each additive. Additives that mainly exert physical actions can significantly promote the cooling process of lithium battery fires, while the advantage of additives that undergo chemical reactions lies in strengthening the inhibition of temperature rise.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the inhibitory effect of composite additives on lithium battery fire
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 shows the temperature changes of lithium battery fires under the action of various composite additives. Under the action of composite additives, except for the intense thermal runaway stage, the temperature is below 200℃. It can be clearly observed that reignition occurs in the composite group of FeCl2 and urea, while the temperature change in the FeCl2 + Tween 20 combination is the gentlest. The temperature reaches a peak of about 550°C at around 300 s and then decreases. The reason is that although urea can achieve cooling and oxygen isolation through endothermic decomposition, its reaction rate is relatively slow. It cannot respond in time at the beginning of thermal runaway, and the moment when it exerts a cooling effect is relatively delayed. Tween 20, through its relatively mild physical modification ability, improves the distribution and evaporation efficiency of droplets, directly altering the fine water mist itself without requiring a thermal reaction. This achieves limited but effective synergistic suppression. This fully illustrates that the introduction of different additives may produce completely different regulatory effects. Their actions are not simply a superposition of performance but depend on the dynamic coupling of the additives' own physicochemical behaviors with the thermal environment. In combination, the "mode of action" and "response rhythm" of additives are more crucial than their "effect intensity" itself. In this paper, the combination with Tween 20 exerts the optimal suppression efficiency in both the temperature rise stage and cooling stage of thermal runaway through the mechanism of "chemical interruption + moderate physical enhancement." It not only significantly delays the temperature rise process and effectively reduces the peak temperature but also avoids the risk of secondary heat release.

Fig. 9 shows the temperature changes during the period of intense thermal runaway. The data indicates that the temperature of the FeCl2 + urea group rises rapidly; the temperature rise of the Tween 20 group is gentle, showing excellent suppression ability; while the temperature rise of the urea composite group is faster. The reason is that both sodium lactate and FeCl2 can ionize corresponding ions to capture free radicals in the combustion chain, interrupting the thermal runaway chain reaction from a chemical perspective. When used alone, they already have a strong suppression effect. When compounded with Tween 20, the latter, as a surfactant, optimizes the wettability and spread ability of fine water mist, enabling water droplets to more easily wet the surface of the battery and penetrate the interior. This enhances the evaporation and heat - absorption efficiency without interfering with the chemical action. The two form a good synergy, significantly delaying the temperature rise. When compounded with urea, due to its lag in reaction kinetics, it fails to take effect in time, resulting in a relatively faster temperature rise. This further verifies that the "mode of action" and "response rhythm" of additives in combination are more critical than their "effect intensity" themselves.

Fig. 10 shows the highest temperature of the battery after the application of fine water mist under different additive application strategies. As shown in the figure that without additives, the highest temperature under the action of fine water mist alone exceeds 1000℃. After applying a single additive, the highest temperature of the battery is close to 850℃, a decrease of over 15 %, indicating that adding additives to fine water mist can significantly enhance the cooling and suppression effect. After applying composite additives, the highest temperature of the battery further decreases. Among them, the combinations of FeCl2 + Tween 20 and sodium lactate + Tween 20 show the best effects, with temperatures around 650℃, which is about a 35 % improvement in suppression effect compared to fine water mist alone. It can be seen that adding composite additives to fine water mist can significantly enhance the suppression effect on lithium battery fires. Compared with other composite groups, the Tween 20 series show the most excellent performance in composite additives.

Table 5 summarizes the reignition cases of lithium batteries under different conditions during the experiments. Reignition is caused by the residual heat inside the battery and the uneven cooling due to the temperature difference between the inside and outside [33]. Fine water mist cools the surface quickly, but the inside still retains heat due to poor heat conduction. When the application of fine water mist is stopped, the internal heat is transferred to the surface, causing a temperature rebound. It can be seen from the table that reignition occurred in the experiments with fine water mist and fine water mist with a single additive. Except for the combination of FeCl2 + urea, there was no reignition in the composite additive experiments. The reignition in the FeCl2 + urea combination may be because the chemical action of FeCl2 is relatively strong while the effect of urea is somewhat delayed. When the fine water mist was stopped, the cooling effect of urea had not yet reached its best. The fine water mist with composite additives rarely reignites because its multiple components work together, offering both efficient cooling and chemical inhibition. This can completely eliminate residual heat and block reignition conditions. In contrast, a single additive has only one function and its cooling isolation effect is insufficient to prevent internal heat transfer, leading to temperature rebound and reignition. The FeCl2 + urea group may reignite because FeCl2 dissociates Fe²⁺ ions with strong reducing properties, providing rapid chemical inhibition, while urea has a delayed effect. When the fine water mist is stopped, urea's cooling effect hasn't reached its peak, creating a time difference between the two. Overall, composite additives more effectively suppress lithium - battery fires.

This study takes lithium batteries as the research object and conducts experiments on the suppression of lithium battery thermal runaway. It compares and analyzes the effects of different additive application approaches on the efficiency of fine water mist in suppressing lithium battery thermal runaway. The conclusions are as follows:
1. The state of charge of lithium batteries significantly influences their thermal runaway risk and combustion intensity.

The moment of thermal runaway occurs earlier as the SOC increases, while the peak temperature decreases correspondingly. The combustion behavior evolves from merely releasing smoke to jet flames and even deflagration. Essentially, a higher SOC enhances the reactivity of battery materials, accelerating the exothermic reactions during thermal runaway. The suppression process of lithium battery fires by fine water mist alone can be divided into four stages: energy accumulation, thermal runaway suppression, reignition, and natural cooling. The reignition phenomenon is due to the temperature difference between the "cold outside" and "hot inside" conditions. The residual heat inside the battery is transferred to the surface after the mist is stopped, causing a temperature rebound.
2. Each additive has an optimal mass fraction, and the advantageous stages of different types of additives are different.

The optimal mass fractions of FeCl2, sodium lactate, urea, and Tween 20 are 0.15 %, 2.5 %, 0.3 %, and 0.3 %, respectively. Additives that exert physical effects (urea and Tween 20) enhance the suppression effect by strengthening the physical properties of fine water mist. Additives that undergo chemical reactions (FeCl2 and sodium lactate) improve the suppression effect by ionizing corresponding ions to interrupt chain reactions. Compared with each other, chemically reactive additives are better at inhibiting the temperature rise during thermal runaway, while physically acting additives perform better in the cooling stage.
3. When additives are combined, their "mode of action" and "response rhythm" are more critical than their "effect intensity" itself. Composite additives can effectively suppress reignition.

Composite additives enhance the suppression efficiency of fine water mist through the synergistic mechanism of "chemical interruption + physical enhancement." Tween 20 improves the distribution and evaporation efficiency of droplets without requiring a thermal reaction, achieving the best synergistic suppression effect. Under the action of composite additives, except for special cases (FeCl2 + urea), fine water mist can effectively suppress reignition, while fine water mist alone and fine water mist with a single additive cannot avoid reignition. Composite additives eliminate the "temperature difference between inside and outside" through the synergy of multiple components - chemical action blocks internal heat - generating reactions, and physical action cools down, completely removing residual heat. In contrast, a single additive has only one function (e.g., FeCl₂ only chemically inhibits, and Tween 20 only physically cools), which cannot solve the problem of internal heat storage, hence reignition occurs.

The conclusions of this experiment can provide theoretical support and technical references for the safety management of lithium batteries or the emergency fire response of related lithium battery equipment (such as drone batteries, charging packs, and energy storage cabinets). In addition, to eliminate the influence of SOC on the peak temperature during thermal runaway and to better observe the combustion phenomena, the SOC of the batteries used in the experiment was 100 %. Future research could focus on the effects of additives on lithium batteries with different SOC levels.
Tian Tingting: Investigation. Xu Qinkun: Funding acquisition. Xia xin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
